segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: We need to remember that less is more.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: So don't come with 50 numbers.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: It's not going to work for the business discussion.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Focus on what really matters and always show how that aligns with the business.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: So if you're talking about predictability, yeah, this project is landing on time or 90 percent of it is landing on time.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: And here's why, and here's the choices that I've made.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: That was when you start overcoming those glazed eyes of oh, some numbers on something that I don't understand.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: You're seeing success when you get, people are asking you to drill down.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Right.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: They're asking you smart questions about the data that you're showing.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Hey, what's up, everyone?

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Welcome to another Labs episode of Dev Interrupted.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: I'm your host, Dan Lines, LinearB COO and co founder.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: And in our Labs episodes, we dive deep into data.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: And best practices that we find.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: And today we're talking engineering goals and reporting.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: So what we're going to do, we're going to unpack the data and strategies that elite engineering organizations use to set goals that drive predictable software delivery, and also how you can report on your progress to senior leadership.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Very, very important thing to do.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: And I'm joined by a voice, who will be, I think, very familiar to many of our listeners, an expert who has helped countless organizations use goal setting to drive engineering improvement, LinearB's own CTO, Yishai Berry.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Yishai, welcome to the pod.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Thanks Dan.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Great being here.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Awesome to have you on, brother.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: We're going to dive right in.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Our first topic in general is around goal setting in the dual mandate.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: I know our listeners are likely aware of the meanings of some of these acronyms that we're going to use here.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: There's a bunch of them, but I think they're crucial to the topic we're about to discuss.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: And so just to make sure we're aligned, I want to quickly Quickly maybe recap some of this terminology, so we'll probably talk OKRs, so OKRs, Objectives and Key Results, KPIs, Key Performance Indicators.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: These are like the most business oriented acronyms ever.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Then we have SMART goals, S M A R T, which are Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time bound goals.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: So those might be some of the things that You know, some of the acronyms that we use.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Now, one of the key things we've noticed in goal setting for engineering teams is a shift in what goals need to look like.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: So engineering leaders today have a dual mandate of both expected to be elite in operational excellence.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: So when we think of operational excellence, I almost think of this as like maybe where engineering teams started.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: It's to deliver the stuff in high quality.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Do your engineering thing.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: We've been doing this, I don't know, 50 years now, 30 years now, ever since I was doing engineering.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: That's operational excellence.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Like, be really good at engineering stuff.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: But the other side of it, and I think this is newer, I know it's newer.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: And I know the best engineering leaders talk to me about it all the time.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: A lot of our customers are doing this.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: It's alignment with business priorities.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: That's the second part of the dual mandate.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: And I think the reason is most organization has recognized, okay, our engineering team is like the lifeblood of the company.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: There's a product that goes along with it.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: We sell that product.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Maybe it's a SaaS product.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: And if we're aligned on business objectives, we make more money.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: I think that's where it's coming from.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: And that's the other side of it.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: So I'm going to, that's kind of setting the table.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: So Yishai, how has that dual mandate impacted how R& D leaders need to think about goal setting?

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Yeah.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: So definitely a shift, like you said, if you, you, you go back, um, you know, when I started working in engineering management and leadership, there was always the focus of, Are we running a tight ship?

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Are we getting the most out of our developers and that spend?

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Are we, um, effective in, um, translating the, you know, the cost of having a dev team into new features?

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Velocity?

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: There's a very old word in engineering management, um, but also quality.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Are we good at what we do as a, as a, as an art, if you like?

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Um, and I think the strong engineering leaders always had the notion of being aligned with what the business needs, but it's more recent that this is surfacing as something that's measurable and something that needs to be, you know, Tied to how we set goals, how we measure our organization, ourselves, how we report to our peers to, you know, managing up.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: That is a little more recent and there's good ways to think about, are we aligned with the business?

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Are we not just running a tight ship?

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: In an effective operation, we're also, um, tuned to what the business needs, which means it's, you know, this is about delighting customers.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: It's about understanding the things that move the needle for the business and for the business to grow or to take the next step in its journey.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Um, we know software is eating the world and every company now is a software company.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: I think a lot of that shift came from that.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: It's no longer one of the departments which happens to, you know, dabble with writing code for some either internal or even a product.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: All the companies are becoming software companies.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: If you run an airline, you're a software company, right?

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: If you're selling hamburgers, you're a software company.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: So, um, having the engineering department, you know, it's rapidly becoming stronger.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: More of the spend is going there.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Decisions which used to live outside, like in IT, or even in other business processes, have become engineering, because engineering, uh, everything is software.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: So now even business processes, in many cases, are run by engineering.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: All that burden now means that we have to really focus on, are we building and investing our time and energy in the right places?

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Which is the business alignment part.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: You know what's so funny about what you were saying?

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: I was thinking a lot about it.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: I think it's really smart.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: And it's almost like this dual mandate, honestly, has kinda been there forever in a sense.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Right now, and for the forever future, it's like mandatory.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Because of all the reasons that you said, every, every company is a software company, even if you sell hamburgers, you're a software company.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Yeah.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: But if I think back to when I first started, even in development, very, very long time ago, the best leaders, people would say, Oh yeah, I love that person.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Oh yeah.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Why do you love that person?

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Well, that person can talk about when is the project going to be delivered?

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: They can talk to the CEO about what the CEO's needs are.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: What are the most important things that they were almost like amazing translator.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: They had all of the engineering practices of efficiency, quality, you know, low change failure rate.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: And they had this other ability to say, okay, I know exactly what the business needs and I can talk to all of the go to market team about how my team engineering is going to hit those goals.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Like those were the best leaders.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Now I think it's just like mandatory to be like that.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Would you agree?

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Like the people you think about who are like, great.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: I think it's, it's both becoming mandatory or a sign of, yeah, this is a strong leader for engineering.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Um, it's also becoming much more explicit.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: It's not just a soft thing that you need to have.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: There's, you need to be talking about very specific, um, items and, you know, interact with the rest of the business in specific ways.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Yeah.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Because if you're not going to be predictable and empower the go to market side of the house Then there's a built in delay between when features and capabilities are ready and when the field can actually sell them, for example, uh, or support them.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: So it's becoming much more structured, I think.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Yeah, you're right.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: I mean, and that I think leads us to the data and the goals.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: So now that it is.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Mandatory, and it needs to be explicit.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: How can engineering leaders set goals that help them achieve both sides of this

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: you know, we're now taking this to a more tangible step of, okay, I need to set goals.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Like in every business unit, I'm not going to measure everything or goal around everything.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: It's too much.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: You need to apply some focus.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Um, realizing and understanding that the dual mandate means that, okay, I need to think about at least the two sides.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: I need goals around my operational excellence, about our productivity.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Um, but I also have to have some goals around the business alignment.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: So, the first, like, um, you know, actionable step is make sure you have a goal on each of each side of that dual mandate.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: A goal on the business alignment part can be around your predictability.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Am I being, like, able to deliver on promises or be predictable about delivery in a consistent way?

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: It could be about, um, are we spending enough on Um, on building new value, are we able to focus enough of our efforts in building and creating new value and not just chasing, uh, putting out fires, uh, just, you know, keeping the lights on?

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Do we have enough of our spin on innovation?

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Or it could be about the key projects, the key initiatives that the business really cares about and making sure that we are investing enough in these.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Not just in our plans, but also in actuals.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: If I needed 20 people on that project, am I actually getting 20 people on that project?

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: These are the kinds of goals we can take for the business alignment side.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: And then on operational excellence, there's, things like cycle time, like understanding our, um, developer experience, our quality.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: There's a bunch of goals and metrics depending on where you are in where what's really important for you as an organization I think these things are a little easier to measure because they've been around longer.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: There's a little more And even on intuition, engineering leaders tend to gravitate to those areas.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: So, have one on each is a good starting point.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Yeah,

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: I think what we want to do, of course, what I want to do on this pod, we have a lot of pods in Dev Interrupted that are about the DORA metrics, cycle time, change failure rate, deployment frequency, MTTR, then you have all your leading indicators, PR size, rework rate, I'm going to put it aside for a second.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Let's talk about where the industry is going.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: And you said the one that caught my ear the most was predictability.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: When I talk to engineering leaders and what I know from my past career, the question that's always asked on the engineering leader is like, when is the thing going to be done?

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Delivered to me.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: When do I get my value?

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: When do I get to close a big deal because you have this new feature that came out?

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: When do I get to retain a customer?

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: So if you don't mind, let's focus in on predictability.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: And one of the, you know, KPIs that we talk about at LinearB would be the planning accuracy.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: It sounds cool, and I think it's a great KPI.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Maybe you can start there, but what are the other things that come to mind for you when you think, Delivering projects on time, predictability.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Yeah.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: So I'll start by saying, you talk with engineering leaders and with, with business leaders, the CEOs and you know, the peers around the table, like the marketing leaders, the sales leaders, and As you look, as you look into more larger companies, larger businesses, uh, forward thinkers, they are starting to trade volume or velocity for predictability.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Right?

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: I can take a little less production, but at a better predictability.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Right?

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Why?

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Because software is, you know, going agile.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: It's all about trying to move fast, even break things.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Tight learning cycles, but then there's the whole surrounding part of the business that needs that predictability.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: You're not going to be able to pull up a marketing campaign and Uh, in a day, just because they release the agile software team just delivered, right?

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: You have to prepare.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: You have to line up things.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Um, not everything in the world can be as agile as software delivery.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: So predictability becomes more and more important.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: As you grow as a business, as you have more customers, uh, more varied set of customers, um, and, um, with that in mind, looking at the, the, the, Specific metrics and the specific ways you can measure and goal around predictability becomes really important.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: So, planning accuracy, you know, that's a good place to start.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: This is about understanding how we deliver sprint over sprint.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Is our planning and delivery, are they aligned?

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Are we able to deliver what we promise?

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: First to ourselves, then to our surroundings, um, on a sprint by sprint basis.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: If you're able to master that, you're on a path to being able to predictably deliver a project, which is like multi sprint, maybe a quarter, uh, a bigger chunk of work.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: And then you have, when you look at, you know, you zoom out, you look at a project.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Now you have, um, you can talk about, How much of the project scope did I hit?

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Did I, is it getting, getting in on time?

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: And it's not just about goaling and reporting.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: You can also make some decisions before the project ends to optimize what you're landing with.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: You're not always going to deliver 100 percent of everything.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: We know that.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: There is a time when you look at the data and you say, okay, I need to change something.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Maybe give up on these scopes, some items.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Focus my energy on the ones that are almost there.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: So, understanding the, the burn up, if you like, for a project, or for your whole, your whole delivery in a quarter or similar planning cycle.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Making adjustments, but also tracking quarter over quarter, are we delivering enough of what we promised, enough of what we planned, in good quality.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: That would be a great goal to, to track and improve.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: I mean, you talked about kind of the maturity I would call it, of the space maybe moving from I always have like the CEO in mind'cause I have this like CEO archetype of like, just give me all the stuff.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Like do it fast.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Yesterday, there's, yeah, yesterday, I needed it yesterday.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: A little more maturity of like, okay.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: I value predictability because I understand my marketing team can be in sync.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: My enablement team for sales can be in sync.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: My enablement team for CS.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: The way that we go out with this product matters to me or this feature matters to me.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Maybe you have like a Gartner interview coming, coming up.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: The predictability matters a lot for the whole business to be in sync.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: It's like, I remember the CEO used to ask me, okay, is the project going to deliver on time?

segments.speaker: Dan Lines

segments.body: That's like a very like qualitative conversation.

segments.body: And even better now, I know this is like futuristic stuff, but this is what we're working on with some of our customers.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Let me show you a Monte Carlo simulation that if we were to do this, why I think it will help us deliver on time.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Now I'm talking with data.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: I'm talking with risk levels.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: I'm talking with Simulation to the business of like, let me bring you in of what it really takes to forecast.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: I think that's really cool and kind of like next level mature conversation.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: What do you think about that?

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Yeah, I really like the the ability to take this from uh, is this on time?

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Yes.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: No, or why isn't it on time?

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Right.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: That's the common question.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Why are you guys late?

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: You know, using data, we can move the conversation to let's, let's make some decisions.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: It's probably not just magically create a new resource, but let's move someone from a project that is doing well and maybe has some capacity, uh, that, that they can contribute here because I have a bottleneck here.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: The data is showing me the kind of risk that I can alleviate with moving a resource and maybe not lose so much on the other project because the signals there are different.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: What?

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: When the conversation moves to making choices and you see the choice you see the you know the outcomes it becomes a much Much stronger conversation.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Okay, so let's do a little summary on the predictability side, we have some KPIs, right?

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: We have planning accuracy.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: That's a good one.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: We have capacity accuracy.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: That's a good one.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: We have project forecasting with simulations.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: That's a good one.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: And now imagine having that for all of your projects.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: You see them all in one view.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: That's awesome.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Now there's kind of this like what you're saying.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: I have to make decisions of what, what to do.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Can you talk to us a little bit about goals around resource allocation?

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Cause sometimes a lot of those decisions might be, ah, I don't know.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: We just were understaffed on this project.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Is it really the top project?

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: I could move this one faster if we could delay this other one, but I got to shift some people.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Talk to me about resource allocation, investment profile, goals around that kind of stuff.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: So both resource allocation and investment profile are ways to look at the actual spin.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Where is my energy going?

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Where are my people deployed?

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Not just in, in, on paper, on plan, but what is actually happening?

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: And I think a very common, um, cycle, and we've seen, we've all seen this, where Management sits at the beginning of the quarter, lays out a plan, allocates resources.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Yeah, let's take 20 people for this project.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Let's put this team on that project.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: This is important for us.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: It's a new product or a new feature.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Let's, um, um, concentrate some work and resources there.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Great.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: The plan looks good.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Then reality hits, right?

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: So this 20 people project, yeah, on paper there's, they're all there.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: But three you haven't even hired yet, two are busy, uh, with an old project that they weren't able to ramp down quickly enough.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Um, another one is pulled to put out fires elsewhere.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Reality is different.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: And then the quarter ends and why have we missed?

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: You spend time to realize that actually we didn't have the 20 people.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: So research allocation is a way to look at what is the actual spend across projects or initiatives or any kind of like slicing the pieces of work in your, um, dev work and course correcting.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Yeah, this is what is actually happening.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: This is what we wanted.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: We can now course correct.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: We can help people ramp up more quickly or ramp down more quickly if a project needs that.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Or we realize that we have a gap.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Now we can make a choice.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Okay.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Reality is a bit different.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Let's make a choice about what to, um, you know, what to give up on, what to invest more in and so on.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Totally

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: makes sense.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: And now we can have kind of that educated conversation with the business.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Okay, so now that we talked about kind of both sides of the dual mandate, some of the KPIs that you can use for engineering efficiency, we have our DORA metrics, we have some leading indicators, we talked a lot on the business.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Alignment side, predictability, very, very important.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: I want to ask you, in terms of actually setting goals against these KPIs, where in the org structure should I start?

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Is it like a top down thing, a bottom up thing?

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: What are your, what are your thoughts on that?

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: That's a good one.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: I think, uh, there's probably not one answer.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: first of all, there's the org culture that you have to take into account.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Um, in some, um, respects, and I think mostly on the engineering, uh, like operational efficiency, there's a lot of sense in doing this bottoms up and letting the teams decide on which goals to take and what, like, how to tune the goals.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Because, uh, in many modern, um, you know, development organizations, you want to give autonomy and you have the right people that you can give autonomy.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: The team knows how it's delivering.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: They have, maybe even between the teams, there's a difference in how they're working.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Some could be using scrum, some could be using Kanban, some could be using a combination.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Some are building.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: You know, SaaS front end, some are doing mobile apps.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: So very different ways to measure and to benchmark.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: So giving autonomy and the bottoms up approach makes a lot of sense.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: And also very strong culture, positive of, you know, it's not like a big brother watching over your back and measuring you.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: It's more like This is a way for you to measure yourselves and report up.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Um, and it still makes sense in these cases to have a top down focus.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Yeah, we know as a company, as a dev work, we have, we want to focus on being more efficient.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Let's make sure we look at cycle time top down because it's a good efficiency and bottleneck discovery kind of metric.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Um, if we have a quality issue or a quality, initiative going on in the company.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: We can make that a top down and bake that together with with bottoms up.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Yeah.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: On the business alignment side, I think it's typically running top down with, you know, org level or VP level and then departments or groups.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Aligning either to the product lines or the way the org is built.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Um, you want predictability to be measured across your teams or groups and then aggregate it up.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: That makes a lot of sense coming top down.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: it's kind of like a mix.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: One thing that I've seen, especially with the larger organizations, Let's say you have a hundred developers plus 5, 2 50 developers, 500 developers, and then I don't know, we, I'm working with companies that have 6,000 developers.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: I think that there does need to be something top down around standardization, I'll call it.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Now, of course you want bottom up adoption, but when I think of standardization, it's kind of like, here is how we're going to measure cycle time.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: This is our standard practice of when cycle time starts versus when cycle time ends, and that's the standard for our business.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Here is how we're going to measure sprint planning accuracy.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: This is, you know, how we start it either when one day into the sprint or right when the sprint So, what I've seen is when you bring these top down standards It kind of says, when do things start and stop?

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: And what do the, what are the KPIs that we care about?

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Now, after that, we care.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Yeah, we care.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Now you have the standards.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: We do care.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: I think it's more go deploy this with your teams and set your goals around this.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Here's our high level goals.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: You go make it happen.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: That's where I've seen it works really well.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: You have top down standards.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: You say the KPIs that the company or the business unit cares about, and you give some high level guidance on the goals.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Now you go make it happen for your team.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: That's, that's kind of what I've seen work really well.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: And I think that lets the, the, the teams or the, you know, lower parts of the org focus a little on the leading indicators.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Sometimes on more technical, um, ways to look at the problem.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: So you could, you're thinking about cycle time as a top down, but then for a team to improve their cycle time, they could be looking at smaller PRs.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: They could be looking at how long does it take me to pick up a PR for reviews, a pickup time.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: There are the ways to implement that goal are a little more detailed.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: And another team may look at different, uh, they have different bottlenecks.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: So they'll, they're going to take a goal around a better review process.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Or can I automate away some of my reviews?

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Absolutely.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Now, what I want to do in the last few minutes of this pod, is talk a little bit more about the business stakeholders and what it takes for a VP of engineering, a CTO, a director of a business unit.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Maybe even you're communicating, you know, to the board.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: We have, you know, You know, from LinearB board slides that you can use to communicate, you know, these KPIs.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: But what can you tell us, maybe an overview or some tips and tricks?

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: I'm an engineering leader.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: I'm measuring this kind of stuff.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: How do I approach business stakeholders?

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: I've never done it before.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: What do I, what do I do?

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Yeah.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: So, um, first, you know, a lot of us hit that wall where, uh, it feels like no one cares at the beginning.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: You know, everyone is about the sales numbers and the marketing numbers.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: They're used to seeing numbers and data in measurements for those departments, and they're used to having engineering say I think this feature is gonna land in two months.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: That's, so there is some education, Oh, there's actually good data to look at.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: There's actually a way to drill in.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Um, so come prepared to, to Not just show the data and the goals, but also explain why it matters.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Why cycle time matters so much, because it lets me, um, you know, learn much faster from the, the feedback that I'm getting from our customers when our new, uh, the new code hits the, um, the users.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Um, by reducing these bottlenecks, I can get so much more done and improve the quality.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: So, be, like, be prepared to explain why it matters.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: I think as engineer leaders, and many of us are engineers at heart, uh, we need to remember that less is more.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: So don't come with, you know, 50 numbers.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: It's not going to work for, for the business, uh, discussion, um, focus on what really matters and always show how that aligns with, with the business.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: So if you're talking about predictability, yeah, this project is landing on time or 90 percent of it is landing on time.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: And here's why, and here's the choices that I've made.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Um, I think that, that was when you start, uh, overcoming those, you know, glazed eyes of oh, some numbers on something that I don't understand.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: You know, you, you've, you're seeing success when you get, people are asking you to drill down.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Right.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: They're asking you smart questions about the data that you're showing.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Yeah, that, that's, that totally makes sense.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Especially when you start getting some of that engagement and questions.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: I think my best tip here, what I've seen work really well, if you go to your business stakeholders and say, I'm putting a new thing on your calendar.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: So first of all, it's repetitive and it's going to be repetitive and it's going to be called our forecasting meeting, our project forecasting meeting, and it's monthly, all you got to do is show up.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Now, one thing that's cool about that.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: You may already have this.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: You know, ceremony of a forecasting meeting, you may not.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: So you're going to get some, if you don't, some kudos points, because those stakeholders are going to say, I definitely want to come to a project's forecasting meeting because that's what I care about.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Now's your opportunity.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: You got them in that meeting to start hitting them with, is the project going to deliver on time?

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Let me show you some of the underlying engineering metrics of cycle time or change failure rate.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: You have the planning accuracy, and maybe you start getting some questions about that.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: And usually what I see is if you relate it back to what they care about, which is these business deliverables, that is when you'll get, Oh, now I care about, Oh, cycle time has something to do with me getting what I want.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Let me ask a question about it.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Yeah.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: So that would be my tip, my biggest tip.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Have you seen that kind of stuff work or any comments on that?

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Yeah.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: I think you talk about cycle time in, in, um, you know, in isolation.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: No one cares.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Yeah.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: You talk about cycle time affecting delivery of this project, which is What I need to sell more as a sales leader.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Now I can start relating and you'll be surprised by the kind of smart questions you get from your business peers about the data and about what it means.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Just like you can ask about the sales cycle and how is the sales cycle different in different segments, right?

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: It's natural for us to drill in when we see the connection.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Yeah, it's so funny.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: It's like, uh, Hey, I want to go ask for new hires for engineering.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: The answer is always no.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Do more with what you have.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: We're in an efficiency mode.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: But if you could ever get a question that says, Oh, we're talking about the delivery of this project.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: I'm showing you why we're probably not going to deliver.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: It's because we have a poor cycle time due to a reviewer bottleneck and you can get your business stakeholders to say, Oh, Well, what if we had another reviewer?

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Oh, great.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Perfect.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Yes.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Cause I would love to add another reviewer.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Actually, I need a senior one.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: I have a job description.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Should we deploy that to deliver this project on time?

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: That's cool.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Or, or even I can show you through the metrics that this project is very efficient.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: It's already running very efficiently.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: There's LinearB publishes industry benchmarks across all of our metrics.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: We are.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: actually doing very good in this project, which means if you give me more resources, there's like, they can be very effective here.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Another person in this project can really kill it because I've already removed a lot of the bottlenecks.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Yes,

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: it's now running very effectively.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: A new resource is going to be very effective here.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Let me prove to you that it will work.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: So we're coming to the end here.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Could you tell us a little, so we have a CTO board report that can be used.

segments.speaker: Dan Lines
segments.body: Talk to me about this CTO board report.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Yeah, so this is a template that, you know, you can use as a, as a starting point to put some, pull some data from LinearB or however you measure your, uh, your key goals, your KPIs.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: And, you know, in basically two or three slides, convey the key information to, you know, the board or the staff meeting and similar.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Uh, Senior Leadership.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: And we're basically splitting it into two parts.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: One is a heartbeat of the health of the engineer organization.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: The key, key, um, metrics, the top downs.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: This could be cycle time and something, you know, merge frequency for developer experience.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Two or three metrics with a trend, with an industry benchmark.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Um, um, Context, so you anchor the numbers.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: It's not just three on the, on the, on the screen.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: It's a three, which means we're in the, I don't know, strong band of the benchmarks.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: So, let's have everyone understand what it means.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: And, we, we, we, uh, suggest that you take a goal.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: You're saying, in Q1, my cycle time was three days.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: My goal for the next quarter is to like lower it down to two and a half days.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: By having this simple cycle of there's a number, there's a context and a trend and I'm taking a goal.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: You are now really helping your peers and your, your managers trust you that you've, you've got this.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: I know what I'm doing.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: I can see where I'm going.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: I have a plan, but I don't think they really care if it's going to be two and a half days or 2.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: 3 days.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: You have a plan.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: You know what you're doing.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: They are, the confidence is there.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: And then the second part is.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: These are the key investments, the key projects.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: We talked about resource allocation, so show some data.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: There's always going to be the top three, four, five, maybe seven projects or initiatives which are what everyone thinks about.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: They are the key, uh, investments.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
segments.body: Show the investment, show that it's aligned to, to, um, the priorities.

segments.speaker: Yishai Beeri
